speaker: interviewer
text: The liberator is Kurt Klein, and the date is December 7, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt, we left off on the last tape with discussing the changes in Germany as Hitler rose to power, the effects it had on your father's business. And I wanted to just backtrack a little bit to ask you a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you had some experiences with your non-Jewish friends that, as Hitler rose to power, the relationships changed. Would you comment on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was, of course, a gradual alienation with my, with my non-Jewish friends and, and classmates. And whereas, in the beginning, they were almost apologetic about it and saying things such as, well, Hitler doesn't mean people like you, really, or your parents. But you will admit there are certain Jews who really deserve to, you know, to, to get Hitler's wrath and so on. In the beginning, they would still be half apologetic about it. This soon turned into a real taunting of, of Jewish boys and, and girls. They might say, you know, there are now some concentration camps. And if you don't behave, and you, if you don't watch it, you'll wind up in one of those. And gradually, they even stop talking to us all together. And-- but I, I, I could-- I had seen the gradual change of that, also saw how they were exposed to Nazi propaganda. For instance, it, it became mandatory for, for all schools and all classes to attend such films as the Leni Riefenstahl film, And I myself had to attend it also. And I could see how, how they were swallowing that up and how, how it affected them and how they were imbued with this idea of German glory. And anything they would tell them about the Jews, of course, they also swallowed whole.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- so then I remember coming out of this film and seeing, having seen the reaction of my classmates, walking along and, and thinking, how did I get into this position? I didn't do anything. Why is all this venom directed at me and my family and all the people I know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I, I could see the role that propaganda can play and how it, how it can influence people. And, of course, the Nazis used propaganda as no one else had done until [? them ?]-- then. As I was growing up, you know, radio came into its own. And Hitler knew exactly how to manipulate the masses on the radio. And he knew how his messages-- his vitriolic messages-- would affect them, and, and how he could, you know, pin the blame for everything that was wrong with the economy and with Germany on, on the Jews. So they became-- Jews became the scapegoat. And so through radio and through film, the Nazis-- that was a great tool for them to, to get to where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you having to sit in a class and watch a film like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it, it was a totally shattering experience for me, to, to find that all these people were turning away from me. And what was even worse, that, that some teachers were espousing those, that same ideology. I remember a, a gym teacher of ours giving a lecture once in, in class to the effect of-- this is as close to what he said as I can remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are certain elements among us here who are merely guests in this country. They will be treated OK as long as they behave themselves. But unfortunately, they have not always behaved themselves, and therefore, we cannot guarantee what will happen to them. I mean, this completely undid me. And not, because, perhaps, even of the content, but because I could see that the Nazis were reaching everybody, not only my classmates. Or the, the, the Nazis also had a, sort of a, a way of, of, of doing certain things. For instance, in the early years, I remember they were marching at, at night sometimes. And when they would come to a Jewish house, they would sing their, their hate songs to the effect-- one, one of them I remember-- when Jewish blood splatters from our knives, then things will go twice as well. That was one of their famous songs. And they would always time it so that they would sing those stanzas when they were marching by a Jewish home. [? Or ?], I remember, for instance, in the early time after Hitler's assumption of power, my religious school teacher, by that time one in Heidelberg, saying to the class of Jewish students, well, we have witnessed a great upheaval recent, in recent times within Germany. We saw it coming but were unable to stop it. For the time being, all we can do is hope that nobody comes to our doorstep and slits our throats.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you having your non-Jewish friends no longer being friendly with you? How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was, of course, a, a great disillusionment for me. And, and you sort of made the best of it. It meant that I, I would spend a great deal more time at home, not associating with anybody because I was physically removed from many of my friends in, in Heidelberg. And so I would do what was still possible. I mean, books were always-- it was always possible to read. Radio, in those days, we could still listen to. Only later, that was prohibited. And, and so I would concentrate on things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What attempts-- were there any attempts made by your family to, to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by '35, roughly, it, it had become evident that we better start the ball rolling and, and try to do something. That meant, of course, that the younger members of our family would leave first. And since my sister was at, at the right stage of her training, she, she made the first attempt to leave. And through the help of some relatives in, in this country, in Buffalo at the time, she got the necessary affidavits and, and was able to come here in '36. And as soon as she got here, she made it her business to prevail on, on some other relatives to send papers for me as well. And she was successful in that. And also, this time slot happened to be a window during which it was still comparatively easy to get an American visa. Because this was the period before Kristallnacht. And so I, I was able to come in-- to the states in 1937. And my brother then followed me the next year in '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances under which you left? In other words, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do you remember leaving your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and that last--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Well, I remember, of course, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tremendously emotional farewell with my mother. It, it so happened that my father had decided to accompany me to Hamburg, from where I would take the ship that would take me to New York. And my, my brother also joined us. He was not living in, in Waldorf. He was, of course, as I said, away in, in Breslau at the time. But he joined us, also came to Hamburg. But I, I remember, of course, the feelings I had. And we left on the note that, of course, we, the younger people, would establish ourselves in this country. And then we would see to it that our parents would also come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a chest of some of my belongings, a lot, a lot of books, some clothes, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few mementos of my parents' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] home, and a, a, a few silver spoons, things like that. By and large, most of it was the clothes I brought and, and the books I, I couldn't part from. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] want to mention this, this much, that my brother told me, many years later, after we knew already my parents' fate, that when my father had seen me off at the dock, or where the boat was leaving from, he turned to my brother and he said, who knows whether I will ever see him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship that you went on? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. It so happens that my brother worked for that steamship company, which was the Hamburg America Line. And, and the name of the ship was which means Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with people that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none I knew. But I found very congenial company, including a, an American student who had been studying in Heidelberg and with whom I had a correspondence following my arrival here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your voyage like to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I considered it something of an adventure. You know, once the farewells were over with, I could concentrate on just-- I, I had never done anything like this, obviously, and I, I had traveled very little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] within Germany. And so this was a great adventure. And here, I was also going to this country that I had read about and seen so many films about. And, and, and that served to distract me in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make some friends on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this American student. And I made some other casual acquaintances, nothing lasting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you came to America? Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I went directly to Buffalo, New York. That's where my sister lived at that time, although she didn't live there for, for very much longer after that. She moved to New York City. But, but I decided to stay because I, I stayed with an uncle and an aunt of mine who, who had come to Buffalo two, three years earlier. They were among the first ones to leave. We also had many native American relatives in Buffalo.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Buffalo, what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed with an aunt and an uncle. On your mother's side or your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Buffalo? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I had started in the printing business in Germany, until the authorities made it impossible for me to continue that apprenticeship. And I had found some interim work in a Jewish factory, just until I, I, I, I-- just to fill the time until I would leave. But once I got to Buffalo, I wanted to get back into printing. And although those were tough times, difficult times-- it wasn't easy to find a job-- I did find one. And I, I started as a typesetter in a, in a printing firm in Buffalo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to talk about some of the bad experiences that were happening in Germany to the people in Buffalo? Were people listening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to convey how serious the situation was and that, that something had to be done. But it was very difficult to reach people with that message. Usually, the answers we would get is, well, first you-- meaning the kids-- establish yourselves. And, in fact, nobody had a really good job in those days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And obviously, there wasn't much money, but-- so in the first year or two, we, we would listen to that. And there was a certain logic to it. But after Kristallnacht, all this changed, and-- but we could not get through to most people the gravity of the situation back in Germany, as far as Jews were concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, were you receiving letters from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I, I have most letters that I ever received in the past. I have some 400 letters from the day I, I left my parents' house to the last letter I got from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things that they were telling you in the beginning? Were they discussing still what was going on in Germany in the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they were discussing it. But they also improvised a certain code in, in which they-- I mean, it was a fairly innocuous code-- but, but in which they would let us know that things were really going from bad to worse. I remember a letter, early on, in which my-- and, and we must have agreed on, on, on that name-- in which my mother said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as Aunt Dora's illness is concerned, I am afraid the prognosis is not very good. It will only get worse, and-- a fact we all regret very much. And in, in my case, I, I am particularly sorry because we had such amiable times with her and, and, and, and really had a good time with her. And, and it's too bad that it had to come to this. And that meant, of course-- Aunt Dora was the code word for anti-Semitism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And later on, they would find other ways of describing things that they felt might go through censorship and they didn't want to say openly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- had you devised somewhat of a code before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I only remember that we devised this Aunt Dora thing. The other things became obvious, and we had to sort of figure them out as, as we went along. But, in fact, in going over those letters so many years later-- let's say 40 to 45 years later-- it was even more difficult to remember what my mother might have meant by certain things. But I can, I can tell you one other that will give you an idea of, of what she said. I remember getting a letter in 1938 in which she said, lately Mr. Stresemann has surfaced again here, but I'm afraid to absolutely no avail. His activities are making no impact here at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, if you don't remember who Mr. Stresemann is, ask your sister. She, she will know. And the reason why she said that-- I thought about this, and I-- at first I didn't know, was there really a Mr. Stresemann in, in Waldorf, or did she mean something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, of course, my sister was no longer alive so many years later. But then it came to me. Stresemann was, of course, a chancellor of, of, of Germany. And when I looked up some of the things he had done, I found that he had, that he had won-- in the Weimer Republic-- that he had won the Nobel Peace Prize for his efforts with the League of Nations and in trying to, to establish peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my mother's way of letting us know that here it was '38, the time when Neville Chamberlain went to Munich and made, made his peace overtures, to let us know that absolutely no one was paying any attention to what he was trying to do within Germany. When did you enter the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in November of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was drafted, since I, I couldn't volunteer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] technically being considered an enemy alien. Which also meant that I couldn't own a camera or have a, a shortwave band on my radio. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But other than that, there were no, no real restrictions. But it-- because there were no distinctions made between Jews and ordinary Germans, I simply fell into that category. And so-- but they did eventually get around to me, and so I was drafted. And when I came to the induction center, sure enough, the post intelligence officer called me into his office and, and sort of gave me a quiz on, on my feelings about this thing and how difficult it might be for me to fight the people I grew up with. But I could assure him that, in view of all the things that were happening-- and by that time, plenty of things had happened to me personally and to my parents-- that I had absolutely no qualms about fighting this evil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you receive your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: I first was sent to Camp Croft in South Carolina. And I was to get six weeks of basic training there. But after four weeks, they, they called me out and said that-- in those days, they used IBM cards [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that, that would tell your qualifications. They had a sort of-- of course, no computer system-- but the IBM cards were the early forerunners of that, you know, with punched holes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that somebody had detected somewhere that I spoke German. And they wanted, of course, bilingual people like that. And so they called me out and sent me to an intelligence training center in Maryland. How long were you with this intelligent--

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- I, and I-- I was there from January through September of '43, with a, a period in between that I spent on maneuvers in Louisiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the period of about two years, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You entered in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: '42. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was not, I was not drafted until November of '42. And by January, '43, I was sent to this intelligence training center. And by September of that year, they flew us to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when did you leave? September, you said, of '43--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to England? And what was the name of the unit that you were assigned to when you flew to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, this was all intelligence personnel. So we first had to go to a pool, which was a, a camp in the Cotswolds in England. From there, they assigned us to the various units. And it so happened that, in my case, they assigned the team I was with-- we formed teams of six persons-- and the team I was with was assigned to the US 5th Infantry Division, at that time stationed in Northern Ireland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your rank at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was a Master Sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a serial number?

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you with any attachments? Was it called a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was attached to the 2nd Infantry Regiment of the 5th Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the commanding officer was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Colonel [INAUDIBLE]. He had an unusual first name. I think it was something like [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, I didn't know him too well. But I got to know him rather well once, after the, you know, after the invasion and once we got into all the campaigns. And he-- although he was a, a strict disciplinarian, he, he had a, a, a definite human side, too. And he was-- he seemed interested in, in what had gotten me or, or some of the others, to, to, to that point that we found ourselves in. And because we were only attached to the division, we actually had our own autonomy. And, and, and, they, they-- the regiment could tell us certain things to do, but, but they didn't have jurisdiction over us. So that, that was rather nice to enjoy this, this kind of freedom. But he, he was quite-- I found him to be quite personable. And I guess he thought we must have been of some help to him in determining what units were facing us. This was the information I had to get out of interrogation of prisoners of war, which was my assignment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the members of your, your unit? Were you-- did you have friends in the unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I mean, our team was made up of a captain, a lieutenant, a master sergeant, a tech sergeant, and some other sergeants. And, of course, we-- working together closely, we, we did become quite friendly, although there were some shifts in personnel as time went along, and some of them dropped out and replacements came in. But also, with the unit that we were attached to, we really became quite friendly with some of the people that we got to know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] especially the regimental headquarters staff. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] throughout my entire Army experience, I personally did not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] come across any overt anti-Semitism, as some people have said they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews in your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there anyone else from Germany, such as yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, members of, of my intelligence team were, you know, were of course-- most of them were from Germany. And this sometimes led to some rather peculiar and even funny incidents. First of all, when we-- the same type of personnel also made up the teams on maneuvers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when, when we first encountered some of the troops, here we were, some of-- with some of these personnels speaking with heavy German accents. And at the time, the troops absolutely didn't know what to make of us. And-- on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, especially during the Battle of the Bulge much later, some, some of our personnel had quite a bit of, quite some problems identifying themselves as, as American troops and, and being believed by the guards, you know, when asked for the password.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other funny incidents you remember such as this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, well, we sometimes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we sometimes would play some, some games. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For instance, when, when we were in Northern Ireland training the-- we, we were training some of the troops in what they could expect to find once they encountered combat. And so we tried to acquaint them with, with what the German army was like. And we would dress up in German uniforms and also demonstrate some of their weapons and how to dismantle them.
